params.text:
The two-stage test of capacity

When there is genuine doubt about whether a person can make a particular decision, the MCA sets out a two-stage test.

Stage 1 — the diagnostic question: Is there an impairment of, or disturbance in the functioning of, the person’s mind or brain? This might be caused by dementia, a learning disability, a brain injury, a mental health condition, a stroke, delirium from an infection, or even the effects of alcohol or drugs. The impairment can be permanent or temporary.

Stage 2 — the functional question: Does that impairment mean the person is unable to make this specific decision, at this specific time? A person is unable to make a decision if they cannot do any one of the following four things:

  • Understand the information relevant to the decision.
  • Retain that information long enough to make the decision.
  • Weigh up or use the information as part of the decision-making process.
  • Communicate their decision — by any means, including speech, signing, blinking or squeezing a hand.

Failing on any one of these four means the person lacks capacity for that decision — but only if it is caused by the impairment identified at Stage 1.

params.alt: Supporting a person to understand a decision

params.question: During a capacity assessment for consenting to a hip operation, Doreen (who has early-stage dementia) can understand and retain the information, but cannot weigh up the risks and benefits to reach a decision. Under the two-stage test, does Doreen have capacity for this decision?

answers.text: No — being unable to weigh up the information means she cannot make this decision, even though she can understand and retain it.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. A person must be able to do all four things — understand, retain, weigh up and communicate. Failing on any one (here, weighing up) means she lacks capacity for this specific decision.

answers.text: Yes — because she can understand and retain the information, that is enough to show capacity.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Not quite. All four elements are required. If a person cannot weigh up the information, they lack capacity for that decision even if they can understand and retain it.

answers.text: No — her dementia diagnosis alone proves she lacks capacity.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. A diagnosis never proves lack of capacity by itself. You must complete the functional stage — here she fails because she cannot weigh up the information, not simply because she has dementia.

answers.text: It cannot be decided until a doctor signs a form saying she permanently lacks all capacity.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Capacity is decision- and time-specific, not a permanent global label, and the assessment is made by the relevant decision-maker — not by a single form.
